results.proposal_summary: This AFD (Agence Française de Développement) landing page aggregates multiple grant co-financing mechanisms for civil society organizations (CSOs) and NGOs implementing international solidarity and development projects. The mechanisms include the CSO Initiatives Platform (annual grants for French and local CSOs, minimum project size €300,000-€500,000, co-financing up to 80-90%), the Crisis and Post-Crisis Calls for Projects (APCC, €83.5 million committed since 2013), the NGO Sector Innovation Facility (FISONG, suspended since 2023), and the Support Fund for Feminist Organizations (FSOF). Eligible geographies cover all DAC ODA-eligible countries with priority to French cooperation partners. Eligible applicants are French associations, foundations, syndicates, and local non-profit organizations registered in DAC-eligible countries; international NGOs were eligible for FISONG. No currently-open call with a concrete application deadline is presented on this page as of June 2026. The ILO, as a UN intergovernmental organization, does not fall within any of the eligible applicant categories.
results.ilo_match_evidence: While some thematic areas covered (social protection, vocational training, gender equality, labor informality) align with ILO mandate areas, the mechanisms are exclusively designed for CSOs and NGOs as implementing entities, and the ILO is categorically ineligible as a UN intergovernmental organization.

results.exclusion_reason: ILO is not eligible: the call is explicitly restricted to Swiss companies (private sector). The funding objective is private-sector investment mobilisation, not ILO-implementable programmatic work. Co-financing of at least 50% is mandatory.
results.classification_summary: This call is restricted exclusively to Swiss private-sector companies investing in Ukraine's reconstruction, and the ILO — as a UN intergovernmental organisation — is not an eligible applicant type. The call's primary purpose is mobilising private capital for investment-ready production facilities, which is outside the ILO's mandate as a technical assistance and policy implementing partner. Co-financing of at least 50% is mandatory, and individual grants range from CHF 3 million to CHF 10 million.

ILO Eligibility.evidence: The call explicitly targets 'Swiss companies with an established presence in Ukraine' and is described as a 'Swiss private sector' initiative. UN agencies or intergovernmental organisations are not mentioned as eligible applicants.

Funding instrument is grant.evidence: The federal government provides 'co-financing' (non-reimbursable contribution) of up to CHF 50 million for private sector projects, described as grant-like public co-financing.

Grant size above threshold.evidence: Individual project amounts range from CHF 3 million to CHF 10 million, well above the USD 50,000 threshold.

Deadline is future.evidence: The submission deadline is 20 September (2026), which is after today's date of 2026-06-01.

Is open call with active application win.evidence: The call opened on 27 May 2026 and closes on 20 September 2026. A specific application website is referenced (SECO 'Call for Proposals' portal), and the call is described as the 'third call for proposals' with a named deadline.

Funding objective is ILO-implementable.evidence: The call's primary purpose is investment in physical production facilities in Ukraine ('building facilities for long-term production'), explicitly framed as private-sector investment mobilisation. This is not technical assistance, capacity building, or policy work that the ILO could implement.

Is procurement or tender.evidence: This is a call for proposals for project co-financing, not a procurement or consultancy tender.

Requires cofinancing.evidence: Companies are explicitly required to contribute at least 50% of the funding required for their project — co-financing is a mandatory condition.

Requires specific accreditation.evidence: No specific pre-existing accreditation with another body is mentioned as a requirement.

Has consortium leadership rules.evidence: No consortium leadership rules are mentioned in the text.

Applicant origin is restricted.evidence: Eligibility is explicitly restricted to 'Swiss companies with an established presence in Ukraine', limiting applicants to entities registered/based in Switzerland.

results.title: Third Call for Proposals: Private-Sector Investment in Ukraine's Reconstruction (SECO)
results.organization: Switzerland (State Secretariat for Economic Affairs, SECO)

Reconstruction projects in Ukraine implemented in collaboration with the Swiss private sector
funding_restrictions: Applicants must be Swiss companies with an established presence in Ukraine
Companies must contribute at least 50% of total project funding (mandatory co-financing)

results.proposal_summary: SECO (Switzerland's State Secretariat for Economic Affairs) has launched the third call for proposals under its Ukraine country programme 2025–2028, providing up to CHF 50 million in co-financing for Swiss private-sector investment in Ukraine's reconstruction. The call is open from 27 May to 20 September 2026. Eligible applicants are Swiss companies with an established presence in Ukraine; individual grants range from CHF 3 million to CHF 10 million, with companies required to contribute at least 50% of project costs. Supported activities focus on investment in long-term production facilities in Ukraine. Projects are selected via a two-stage process by a joint Swiss-Ukrainian committee. The ILO is not an eligible applicant as the call is exclusively targeting Swiss private-sector companies.
results.ilo_match_evidence: The call focuses exclusively on private-sector investment in physical production facilities in Ukraine and is restricted to Swiss companies, with no alignment to ILO's mandate on labour standards, employment policy, social protection, or decent work.

results.proposal_summary: This page on the United Nations Network on Migration website is titled 'Concept Note Submission' but contains no substantive call information — no donor details, objectives, eligible countries, funding amounts, eligibility criteria, deadlines, or application instructions. It appears to be an empty placeholder page and does not constitute an active or specific Call for Proposals.

The CFC funds projects with loan and other debt instruments (maximum USD 1.5 million for regular projects), which create a repayment obligation for the applicant. Additionally, the ILO is not among the eligible applicant types — the call explicitly targets SMEs, cooperatives, social enterprises, NGOs, and public or private sector entities, with no mention of UN agencies or intergovernmental organisations.
results.classification_summary: The CFC 29th Call for Proposals targets SMEs, cooperatives, social enterprises, NGOs, and public/private sector entities in member countries; UN agencies and intergovernmental organisations such as the ILO are not listed as eligible applicant types. The primary funding instrument is loans and debt instruments, not non-reimbursable grants, which creates a repayment obligation that disqualifies this call on the grant-instrument criterion. These two hard exclusion criteria — ILO ineligibility and absence of a grant component — result in an ineligible determination.

Grant size above threshold.evidence: Not applicable as the funding instrument is loans, not grants. Loan maximum is USD 1.5 million, but this does not constitute a grant.

Deadline is future.evidence: The stated deadline is 1 October 2026, which is after today's date of 2026-06-29.

Is open call with active application win.evidence: The page describes a single specific call — the 29th Call for Proposals — with a concrete deadline of 1 October 2026, application forms available for download, and an active application mechanism.

The CFC funds investment-ready commercial projects via loans, not technical assistance or policy work aligned with ILO mandate delivery.

funding_restrictions: No funding for start-up companies (minimum 3 years operational history required)

No research activities (covered by other international organisations)

results.proposal_summary: The Common Fund for Commodities (CFC) has launched its 29th Call for Proposals, inviting applications from SMEs, cooperatives, social enterprises, NGOs, and public or private sector entities based in or operating within any of its 101 member countries. The call focuses on transformative projects that empower smallholder farmers, strengthen commodity value chains, and promote inclusive local development across thematic areas including regenerative agriculture, local value addition, agtech, financial access, and women entrepreneurship. Funding is provided exclusively through loans and other debt instruments, with a maximum of USD 1.5 million per regular project and up to USD 300,000 under the Fast-Track procedure; no non-reimbursable grant component is available. Applications must be submitted by 1 October 2026. Priority is given to proposals from or focused on LDCs, LLDCs, and SIDCs, as well as projects in the Congo Basin region. The ILO is not eligible as applicants must be SMEs, cooperatives, NGOs, or similar entities, and the funding instrument is a repayable loan.
results.ilo_match_evidence: While the call touches on smallholder livelihoods, women's economic empowerment, and decent work (SDG 8 indicators are referenced), its primary purpose is commercial loan financing to agribusinesses rather than technical assistance, labour standards, or social protection work within the ILO mandate.
